guideline_references: NICE NG12 Suspected cancer: recognition and referral — dysphagia and upper GI alarm features
Cancer Council Australia — Optimal Care Pathway for people with oesophago-gastric cancer
Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care — recognising and responding to acute deterioration (aspiration/sepsis)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who palpated a Virchow's node, auscultated crackles, measured vitals, declared nil-by-mouth and dispatched an ambulance for IV fluids and IV ceftriaxone. The AI Doctor CANNOT palpate, auscultate, measure BP/HR, obtain bloods, or perform endoscopy/imaging; it is scored on recognising the GI alarm-feature constellation plus the aspiration emergency, escalating to an emergency ambulance, instructing nil-by-mouth and upright positioning, and arranging urgent endoscopy/specialist assessment — NOT on diagnosing or excluding cancer remotely.

Malignancy-with-acute-complication: an oesophageal alarm-feature presentation (progressive solid-to-liquid dysphagia, ~15 kg weight loss) presents WITH an acute complication (near-total luminal obstruction, active aspiration, dehydration/hypovolaemia, suspected aspiration pneumonitis) — the case is tiered to the acute complication (emergency), not to the routine 2-week-wait pathway a bare alarm feature would earn.

Red-herring anchor: a long history of heavy smoking and chronic GORD self-treated with OTC antacids invites premature closure on 'just reflux' — the alarm features (progressive dysphagia now to liquids, marked weight loss, aspiration on swallowing) must override the benign anchor.
Age (~68) and sex (male) inferred from 'adult offspring present', heavy-smoking + chronic-GORD background and cachectic malignant wasting — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source case GIH-047.
